rubric_results.requirement: Job status visible with real-time updates
rubric_results.evidence: JobList renders JobStatusBadge with distinct colors for all 7 states (staged, pending, running, paused, completed, failed, cancelled) from status-colors.ts which maps each state to bg/text/ring/dot color tokens. WebSocket via useStepwiseWebSocket pushes TickMessage with changed_jobs, triggering React Query invalidation for instant updates. Special 'Awaiting Input' amber badge shown when has_suspended_steps=true. Stale job warning (amber AlertTriangle) if CLI-owned heartbeat >60s old. Navigation badge shows pending suspended job count in header. AppLayout header shows active/total job counts with animated ping indicator when jobs are running.

rubric_results.requirement: DAG renders with status colors, edges, and step details on click
rubric_results.evidence: FlowDagView (826 lines) implements spring-physics camera with pan/zoom/follow-flow, mouse wheel zoom, drag pan, and touch support (pinch zoom, single-finger pan). StepNode renders executor-specific icons (Terminal/User/Brain/Bot/Layers), status-colored borders and badges from STEP_STATUS_COLORS, attempt counters (n/max), duration display, descriptions, and exit rule tooltips on hover with color-coded actions. DagEdges renders animated Bézier curves — blue when target is running/suspended/delegated, gray when inactive, distinct orange for loop edges — with field labels at midpoints showing truncated value previews. DataFlowPanel provides JSON data inspection with copy button for selected edge fields. Sub-flow and for-each expansion supported.

rubric_results.requirement: Human steps present clear input forms with field types, validation, and submit
rubric_results.evidence: ExternalInputPanel renders schema-driven forms via TypedField supporting 6 field types: str (text input), text (multi-line textarea), number (with min/max), bool (toggle button), choice-single (radio buttons), choice-multiple (checkboxes). Each field has labels, descriptions, optional indicators, and error messages. Prompt displayed in amber text with 80-char width limit. Auto-focus first field, Cmd+Enter to submit. FulfillWatchDialog adds field mode vs raw JSON mode with validateAll() against OutputFieldSchema and pre-populated defaults. CLI side: TerminalAdapter uses questionary for interactive prompts (confirm, select, checkbox, text, number with validation) with PlainAdapter fallback for non-TTY. Ctrl+C during input shows abort menu (retry/suspend/cancel).

rubric_results.requirement: Errors shown clearly with context (step name, error message, timestamp)
rubric_results.evidence: StepDetailPanel displays errors in a red-tinted panel (bg-red-500/10, border-red-500/20) with AlertTriangle icon, 'Error' header, error_category badge in monospace styled pill, full error text in monospace with word-wrap, and copy-to-clipboard button. Run history accordion shows all attempts (newest first) with status badge, attempt number, duration, start/complete timestamps, and exit rule resolution with color-coded action badges (advance=emerald, loop=amber, escalate=red). Component auto-scrolls to Run History when a failed step is selected. AppLayout wraps content in ErrorBoundary with clear fallback UI showing error message and Reload button. Toast notifications via sonner for transient errors.

rubric_results.requirement: Every CLI command has helpful --help text
rubric_results.evidence: build_parser() in cli.py defines 37+ subcommands with descriptive help strings. Root parser: 'Enter the flow state. Portable orchestration for agents and humans.' Each subcommand has concise descriptions (run='Run a flow', validate='Validate a flow file', server='Manage the Stepwise server'). Arguments include defaults, choices, and usage hints (e.g., --input 'Pass input variable (repeatable). Use KEY=@path to read from file.'). Logical grouping: server subcommands (start/stop/restart/status/log), cache subcommands (clear/stats/debug), job staging subcommands (create/show/run/dep/approve/cancel/rm). Dedicated 'docs' command provides deeper reference by topic. Minor gap: no inline examples in --help output, but descriptions are sufficiently clear.

rubric_results.requirement: Editor has YAML editing, live validation, step management, and agent chat
rubric_results.evidence: EditorPage provides multi-panel layout: file tree (left, for directory flows), center with tabbed views (DAG visualization, YAML source editor, file viewer), right panel with step inspector (definition, delete, source editing). YamlEditor uses CodeMirror with YAML syntax highlighting, oneDark theme, JetBrains Mono font, and external sync without cursor jumps. ChatPanel supports 3 agent modes (Claude, Codex, Simple) with NDJSON streaming, collapsible tool activity cards, generated YAML blocks with 'Apply' button, contextual quick actions for selected steps, and permission disclosure. Debounced YAML parsing with live error display. Direct flow execution via 'Run' button. Registry browser for discovering and installing community flows.

rubric_results.requirement: Agent output streams in real-time to UI during execution
rubric_results.evidence: AgentStreamView supports live mode (WebSocket subscription via useAgentStream) and historical mode (useAgentOutput for completed runs). useAgentStream processes AgentStreamEvents (text, tool_start, tool_end, usage), merges consecutive text segments, tracks tool status transitions via ref-based state with version-counter re-renders. UI renders text segments in monospace with tool cards showing categorized icons (Search/Read/Write/Execute), running spinners (Loader2 animate-spin), and completion checkmarks. Token usage progress bar visualization with percentage and cost. Auto-scroll respects manual scroll position (pauses when user scrolls up). Animated blue cursor pulse while agent is live. buildSegmentsFromEvents() enables historical replay from stored events.

rubric_results.requirement: Clear navigation between jobs, editor, settings; breadcrumbs or back links
rubric_results.evidence: AppLayout provides persistent top navigation with three sections: Jobs (LayoutGrid icon + pending badge), Flows (FileCode icon), Settings (Settings2 icon) — each with active state highlighting (bg-zinc-800). Mobile-responsive: icon-only on small screens, labels appear on md+. Minimum touch targets (44px) on mobile. Router defines clean URL hierarchy: /jobs, /jobs/$jobId, /jobs/$jobId/events|tree, /flows, /flows/$flowName, /settings. Legacy /editor routes redirect to /flows. Root / redirects to /jobs. NotFoundPage for unmatched routes. Dynamic tab titles reflect current page with pending count prefix. WebSocket status indicator (green/amber/red dot) and engine status (version, active/total jobs) in header. Working directory displayed. Theme toggle persists to localStorage with system preference detection.

qualitative_summary:
Stepwise's web UI is production-quality with a feature-rich DAG visualization (spring-physics camera, animated data flow edges, touch support), comprehensive schema-driven human input system (6 field types with validation), real-time agent streaming with tool visualization and token usage tracking, and a full flow editor with AI chat assistance in 3 modes. Status visibility is excellent — comprehensive color coding for all states, real-time WebSocket updates, stale detection, and suspended-step badging in navigation. Error presentation is thorough with categorized errors, copy-to-clipboard, auto-scroll to failures, and per-attempt run history.

The CLI is equally polished: 37+ commands with logical grouping (server, cache, job staging), Rich terminal rendering with questionary for interactive input, graceful degradation to PlainAdapter for non-TTY, and a QuietAdapter for scripted use. The IO adapter hierarchy (TerminalAdapter > PlainAdapter > QuietAdapter) ensures good UX across all environments.

Navigation is clean and consistent with responsive mobile support, persistent top-bar, URL-driven routing, dynamic tab titles, and WebSocket connection status. The new-user confusion points were all resolvable — friction was primarily in README documentation (no simple hello-world quickstart, script input mechanics require reading docs) rather than tool UX itself. The user successfully built a flow despite these minor documentation gaps, confirming strong learnability.

No UX rubric items fail. The only area for potential improvement is adding inline usage examples to CLI --help output and surfacing a script-only quickstart tutorial more prominently in onboarding. These are polish items, not blockers for 1.0.
